panel.origin: Return on Investment — a measure of how much profit an investment generates relative to its cost, usually expressed as a percentage. The fundamental yardstick of whether something was worth it.
panel.mixology: Marketing pushed for the new campaign, but the CFO wanted to see the projected ROI before approving the budget.
panel.examples: A 20th-century accounting term that escaped the finance department to become business shorthand for 'was this worth the money?' applied to almost anything.

panel.origin: Key Performance Indicator — a specific, measurable value that shows how well a team or company is hitting its goals. The numbers leadership actually watches.
panel.mixology: Her dashboard tracked three KPIs: monthly active users, churn rate, and average revenue per customer.
panel.examples: Rose to prominence with the data-driven management movement; the word 'key' signals these are the vital few metrics, not every number you could measure.

panel.origin: Two business models: 'B2B' (business-to-business) sells to other companies; 'B2C' (business-to-consumer) sells directly to individuals. They demand very different sales and marketing approaches.
panel.mixology: The software started B2C with individual users, then pivoted to B2B to chase larger enterprise contracts.
panel.examples: Shorthand born in the dot-com era, using '2' as a phonetic stand-in for 'to' — a style that also gave us B2B marketplaces and the broader '-2-' naming convention.

panel.origin: The idea that combined efforts produce greater results than the sum of their parts — '1 + 1 = 3.' Often invoked to justify mergers, sometimes more hope than reality.
panel.mixology: The merger was sold on synergy: shared infrastructure and cross-selling that would supposedly make the whole worth more than the two halves.
panel.examples: From Greek 'synergos' (working together). A legitimate concept so overused in corporate decks that it became a punchline for empty buzzwords.

panel.origin: Describes a business or system that can grow revenue rapidly without a matching rise in costs. The holy grail for investors — growth that doesn't get more expensive.
panel.mixology: Software is scalable: the thousandth customer costs almost nothing to serve, unlike a restaurant that needs another kitchen.
panel.examples: From 'scale' (size, extent). The term exploded with software, where serving more users barely raises costs — the dream investors pay a premium for.

panel.origin: 'Vertical' integration or markets means going deep within one industry or owning your supply chain; 'horizontal' means expanding across industries or to competitors' offerings.
panel.mixology: They grew vertically by acquiring their own supplier, then horizontally by adding an unrelated product line.
panel.examples: From geometry's axes: 'vertical' moves up and down a single industry's chain; 'horizontal' moves sideways across markets. A staple of strategy vocabulary.

panel.origin: The unique strength or capability that a company does better than anyone else — the thing it should build around and not outsource.
panel.mixology: Advised to stick to their core competency, they sold off the side ventures and refocused on what they did best.
panel.examples: Coined by management theorists Prahalad and Hamel in a landmark 1990 article, it became central to corporate strategy thinking in the decade after.

panel.origin: Anyone with an interest in a company's outcomes — not just shareholders, but employees, customers, suppliers, and communities. Broadens 'who matters' beyond the owners.
panel.mixology: The new policy weighed every stakeholder: investors wanted profit, employees wanted stability, and customers wanted lower prices.
panel.examples: A deliberate play on 'shareholder' (one who holds shares), widening it to anyone holding a 'stake' in the outcome — central to debates about corporate purpose.

panel.origin: 'Revenue' is all the money coming in from sales; 'profit' is what's left after subtracting all costs. A company can have huge revenue and still lose money.
panel.mixology: The startup boasted $10M in revenue but zero profit — every dollar earned was spent on growth.
panel.examples: 'Revenue' from Old French 'revenir' (to return, come back); 'profit' from Latin 'profectus' (progress, advance). The gap between them is the whole game.

panel.origin: The percentage of revenue left as profit after costs. 'Gross margin' subtracts the cost of goods; 'net margin' subtracts everything. Higher margins mean a healthier business.
panel.mixology: Luxury goods run on fat margins, while groceries survive on razor-thin ones and make it up on volume.
panel.examples: From the literal 'margin' (edge) — the slim edge of money remaining after costs are stripped away. The narrower it is, the less room for error.

panel.origin: The actual movement of money in and out of a business over time. A profitable company can still fail if cash doesn't arrive when bills are due.
panel.mixology: On paper they were profitable, but a cash-flow crunch — customers paying late — nearly sank them before payday.
panel.examples: A plain compound of 'cash' and 'flow', emphasizing timing over accounting profit. 'Cash is king' became the mantra precisely because of this distinction.

panel.origin: Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization — a measure of a company's core operating profitability, stripping out financing and accounting effects.
panel.mixology: Investors focused on EBITDA to compare the two firms' raw operating performance, ignoring their different debt loads.
panel.examples: An acronym from 1980s leveraged-buyout finance, designed to show a company's earning power before the distortions of how it's financed and taxed.

panel.origin: A financial statement showing what a company owns (assets), owes (liabilities), and the difference (equity) at a single point in time. A snapshot of financial health.
panel.mixology: Her first move was to read the balance sheet, checking whether assets comfortably covered the company's debts.
panel.examples: Named because the two sides must 'balance': assets always equal liabilities plus equity. One of the three core financial statements.

panel.origin: How easily an asset can be converted to cash without losing value. Cash is perfectly liquid; a building is not. Also a company's ability to cover short-term obligations.
panel.mixology: The fund held too many illiquid properties, so when investors wanted out, it couldn't raise cash fast enough.
panel.examples: From 'liquid' (flowing freely) — liquid assets 'flow' easily into cash, while illiquid ones are stuck. The metaphor runs through all of finance.

panel.origin: An accounting method that records revenue and expenses when they're earned or incurred, not when cash actually changes hands. The standard for serious businesses.
panel.mixology: Under accrual accounting, the December sale counted in December's books even though the customer paid in January.
panel.examples: From Latin 'accrescere' (to grow, increase). It captures economic activity as it accrues, giving a truer picture than simple cash-in, cash-out accounting.

panel.origin: Spreading the cost of a long-lived asset (like equipment) across the years it's used, rather than expensing it all at once. Reflects wear and aging on the books.
panel.mixology: The $50,000 truck was depreciated over five years, deducting $10,000 annually rather than all at purchase.
panel.examples: From Latin 'depretiare' (to lower in price). It matches an asset's cost to the periods that benefit from it — a foundational accounting principle.

panel.origin: A 'bull market' is rising and optimistic; a 'bear market' is falling and pessimistic. The two animals define market mood and investor psychology.
panel.mixology: After two years of a roaring bull market, the sudden 20% drop signaled the bears had taken over.
panel.examples: Popularly explained by how each animal attacks — a bull thrusts its horns up, a bear swipes its paws down — though the terms' true origins are debated.

panel.origin: A portion of a company's profits paid out to shareholders, usually quarterly. Income from owning stock, separate from the stock's price rising.
panel.mixology: She built a portfolio of dividend stocks, collecting steady quarterly payments regardless of daily price swings.
panel.examples: From Latin 'dividendum' (thing to be divided) — the profit divided among owners. 'Dividend' also survives in math as the number being divided.

panel.origin: Market capitalization — the total value of a company's shares (share price × number of shares). The standard measure of a company's size.
panel.mixology: With a $2 trillion market cap, the tech giant was worth more than the entire economy of many countries.
panel.examples: A compound of 'market' and 'capitalization', it reflects what the market collectively values the whole company at, right now.

panel.origin: Price-to-Earnings ratio — a stock's price divided by its earnings per share. A rough gauge of how expensive a stock is relative to its profits.
panel.mixology: The stock's sky-high P/E ratio meant investors were paying a steep premium, betting on huge future growth.
panel.examples: A cornerstone of stock valuation since the early 20th century, it answers 'how many dollars am I paying for each dollar of the company's earnings?'

panel.origin: Spreading investments across many assets so no single failure can sink the whole portfolio. The closest thing finance has to a free lunch.
panel.mixology: Rather than bet everything on one stock, she practiced diversification across dozens of companies and sectors.
panel.examples: From 'diverse' (varied). The principle 'don't put all your eggs in one basket' formalized into modern portfolio theory, which won a Nobel Prize.

panel.origin: An investment made to offset the risk of another — like insurance for a position. To 'hedge' is to reduce potential loss, often at the cost of some potential gain.
panel.mixology: He hedged his big tech bet by buying options that would pay off if the sector crashed.
panel.examples: From the old sense of a 'hedge' as a protective boundary; to 'hedge your bets' (cover yourself on multiple sides) dates back centuries before modern finance adopted it.

panel.origin: Betting that a stock will fall: you borrow shares, sell them, and aim to buy them back cheaper later. Profitable if the price drops, dangerous if it soars.
panel.mixology: The short-seller borrowed and sold the overhyped stock, profiting handsomely when it later collapsed.
panel.examples: From 'short' meaning being in deficit — you sell shares you don't own (are 'short' on) and must buy them back. The opposite of going 'long'.

panel.origin: Exchange-Traded Fund — a basket of investments (often tracking an index like the S&P 500) that trades like a single stock. A simple, low-cost way to own the whole market.
panel.mixology: Instead of picking winners, she bought a low-fee index ETF and owned a slice of 500 companies at once.
panel.examples: A product innovation of the 1990s; the name describes exactly what it is — a fund that trades (is exchanged) on the market like a stock.

panel.origin: How long a startup can survive before running out of money, based on its current cash and spending rate. Measured in months.
panel.mixology: With $1.2M in the bank and $100K monthly burn, they had twelve months of runway to find profitability or raise again.
panel.examples: An aviation metaphor: like a plane needing enough runway to take off, a startup needs enough cash-distance to reach liftoff before it runs out.

panel.origin: The speed at which a startup spends its cash reserves, usually stated per month. High burn shortens runway and raises the pressure to grow or raise funds.
panel.mixology: Their burn rate doubled after the hiring spree, alarming investors who watched the runway shrink.
panel.examples: From 'burning through' cash — the vivid image of money going up in flames. A core metric venture investors track obsessively.

panel.origin: The estimated total worth of a company, especially a private startup. Set during funding rounds, it determines how much of the company investors get for their money.
panel.mixology: The Series A valued the company at $40M, so the $10M investment bought a 25% stake.
panel.examples: From 'value'. For startups with little revenue, valuation is as much art and negotiation as math — a bet on future potential.

panel.origin: Ownership in a company, usually as shares. Founders, investors, and employees hold equity; its value rises and falls with the company. The currency of startups.
panel.mixology: She took a lower salary in exchange for equity, betting that her slice of the company would one day be worth millions.
panel.examples: From Latin 'aequitas' (fairness, evenness) — your equity is your fair share of ownership. In finance it broadly means ownership value after debts.

panel.origin: Venture Capital — money invested in early-stage, high-risk, high-growth startups in exchange for equity. VCs bet on many companies expecting a few huge wins.
panel.mixology: After bootstrapping for a year, they raised VC to fuel aggressive expansion and hire fast.
panel.examples: From 'venture' (a risky undertaking) — capital for ventures too risky for banks. The modern VC industry grew up alongside Silicon Valley.

panel.origin: A fundamental change in a startup's strategy, product, or market when the original idea isn't working. A controlled change of direction, not a failure.
panel.mixology: The team pivoted from a struggling photo app to the messaging feature users actually loved, and it took off.
panel.examples: From the literal 'pivot' (turning on a fixed point) — the company keeps one foot planted (its team, learnings) while swinging in a new direction. Popularized by the Lean Startup movement.

panel.origin: A privately held startup valued at over $1 billion. Once mythically rare, hence the name; now more common but still a major milestone.
panel.mixology: Reaching a $1B valuation, the startup officially became a unicorn, and the press took notice.
panel.examples: Coined by investor Aileen Lee in 2013 to capture how rare and magical billion-dollar startups then were — like the mythical creature.

panel.origin: The point where a product satisfies a strong market demand — customers want it, use it, and tell others. The make-or-break milestone for any startup.
panel.mixology: Before product-market fit, growth was a grind; after it, demand pulled the product forward on its own.
panel.examples: Popularized by investor Marc Andreessen, who argued nothing else matters until you find it — the moment the market clearly wants what you built.

panel.origin: Borrowed from tech, it means a person's available time and mental capacity to take on work. 'I don't have the bandwidth' is a polite 'I'm maxed out.'
panel.mixology: She wanted to help with the launch but simply didn't have the bandwidth on top of her current projects.
panel.examples: Lifted from telecommunications (the data a channel can carry) and applied to human capacity — one of the most successful tech-to-office metaphor transfers.

panel.origin: 'Low-hanging fruit' means the easiest, quickest wins — the gains you can grab with minimal effort before tackling harder problems.
panel.mixology: Let's grab the low-hanging fruit first: fixing the checkout bug will boost sales faster than any redesign.
panel.examples: From the literal image of fruit easiest to pick because it hangs within reach — a vivid metaphor for the simplest available opportunities.

panel.origin: To return to a topic or person later, after setting it aside for now. A staple of meeting-speak for 'we'll deal with this another time.'
panel.mixology: We're short on time, so let's circle back on the budget question after we've nailed down the timeline.
panel.examples: From the image of looping back around to something — one of the most mocked yet most-used phrases in corporate meetings.

panel.origin: To 'move the needle' is to make a difference big enough to actually register on a gauge — a meaningful, measurable impact, not a trivial one.
panel.mixology: A few extra signups won't move the needle; we need a channel that brings in thousands.
panel.examples: From the image of a dial or meter's needle visibly moving — used to separate changes that genuinely matter from cosmetic ones.

panel.origin: A thorough, detailed examination of a topic, going well beyond a surface overview. 'Let's do a deep dive' signals a focused, in-depth session.
panel.mixology: The quarterly numbers looked off, so the team scheduled a deep dive to find the root cause.
panel.examples: From the diving metaphor of going below the surface — plunging into the details rather than skimming the top of a subject.

panel.origin: Two deadline abbreviations: 'EOD' (end of day) and 'COB' (close of business). Both mean 'by the end of today,' COB leaning toward standard business hours.
panel.mixology: He needed the report by EOD, so she stayed late to send it before midnight.
panel.examples: Office shorthand for setting same-day deadlines; 'COB' descends from banking and trading hours (when business formally 'closes'), while 'EOD' is the looser modern cousin.
